CURRENT AFFAIRS,

This country is so accustomed to being cheated by
Dublin Castle Boards that it is hard for it to pump
up becoming indignation as each fresh robbery is laid
before the people. The Intermediate Commissioners
are the latest culprits. His Grace the Archbishop of
Dublin is one of them—one of the Commissioners that
is, not one of the culprits; indeed, so far from his being
one of the culprits, he has came out in public and has
denounced the robbery. Exhibitions that should have
been awarded have not been awarded. His Grace, who
is a Commissioner and who knows all about it and may
be accepted as an expert in this matter, has made b.he
_charge.. Now, in addition to his Grace, the Lord Chief
Baron, The 0’Connor Don, Father T. A. Finlay, Right
Rey. G. Molloy are also Commissioners. They are only
five. The other Commissioners are:‘—J udge Maddgn,
whoever he is; Rev. W. Todd Martin, a Presbyterian
Minister ; one Samuel Dill, of whom we do not remem-
‘ber to have heard until we looked up his name in the

in the same course in the Junior Grade; and yet every
one of these eight boys is excluded from the list of ex-
hibitioners, and put off with a £3 prize. I think it is
a question which the Christian Brothers of Ireland are
entitled to have answered, Why was the line between
exhibitioners and non-exhibitioners drawn above these
eight boys, and not below them?”’ Well we cannot
authoritively answer his Grace’s question; but as we
have learned to be somewhat suspicious in certain
matters we would suggest in an off-hand way that the
line might have been drawn lower if eight ‘‘saved”’
would have been included as a result.

At the Ennis Quarter Sessions it was sought in vain
to establish a new, or rather resurrect an old, bungery
in Boston. - The license, it appears, had been ‘killed
by prosecutions.”” Father McMahon, P.P., protested
strongly against the granting of a:licence to this house
situate within 20 yards of the Catholic Church gate.
‘We were not surprised to find Father McMahon affirming
that the moral tone of the place had been considerably
improved during the previous two years during which
time the license had been withdrawn. Talk of pride
of race being the curse of Clonakilty, what are we to
think of the kind of pride of the man who told Father
McMahon that he would be ashamed to pass this public-
house without going in. ' The application was happily
rejected by 14 votes to 5; there were four magistrates
who were evidently moral giants like the man who would
be ashamed to pass the bungery ‘without going in
for they did not vote at all; perhaps they were
ashamed !

Newtownhamilton, in the county of Armagh, is a
village with a population of 689. It has 24 public-
houses, and during the past year there were 119 con-
victions for drunkenness, and 13 convictions against
publicans. ‘It would be very hard to die of thirst in
that part of the world,”” said Judge Kisbey. All the
magistrates on the Bench, with the exception of his
Honour, at the recent Quarter Sessions, voted in favour
of an applicant for a new publichouse for Newtown-
hamilton, Co. Armagh!

In the interests of Irish music we hope that Dr. P.
W. Joyce’s generous offer contained in his letter to
Father Gaynor will be taken up at once. Father

" Gaynor, in the course of his recent lecture at Cork, sug-

gested that Dr. Joyce should: be asked to publish -the
rest of his great collection of Irish airs. Dr. Joyce
promptly wrote to Father Gaynor, and in the course

2 o . THE LEADER.

of his letter he said: —T shold be very glad to ‘edit
my collection of Irish airs—or a large AseIZogion from it
—for the Gaelic* League; and in case you—or they
through your suggestion—should follow the matter up,

% please let this be borne in mind: that T do not want,
or need Aany payment, or profit of any kind—except a
few copies” of the book for personmal use. Let the
Gaelic League pay the expense of bringing out the book
—wxf,h my name, of course, on the title page. If they
publish it by subscriptions (as the London-Irish Lite-
rary Society has published Petrie’s collection) I do not
believe they would be at any loss—but, on the contrary,
I am pretty sure it would result in a profit to them.
But T think it should be undertaken by the Central
Body of the Gaelic League, so as to make it thoroughly
representative and ‘influential.””

Now this is a very generous offer and should be closed
\Ylb].\ at once. The Cork Gaelic League has, we be-
lieve, forwarded a resolution to the Executive in Dublin
asking the latter body to take the matter up. A lead-
ing authority on Irish music remarks in a letter to us—
‘“ Joyce’s airs, though less numerous than Petrie’s, are
more typically Irish.”’

The Irish Manure Manufacturers’ Association offer
four prizes of £10, £5, £3, and £2, respectively, for the
four best essays on the following subject— The utility
of 35/37% superphosphate (XXX grass manure) as a

spring  top-dressing, or potassic superphosphate
(equal to 2 parts superphosphate 35/37% and
1 part kainit) as as autumn and -winter top-

dressing to pastures and meadows, used alone or in con-
junction with other manures.” Anyone resident in Ire-
land may enter excepting first prize winners in previous
competitions. All essays must be lodged not later than
15th November next with the hon. secretary, 81 Roger-
son’s Quay, Dublin.

Our angelic contemporary the Belfast News Letter is
beside itself with righteous indignation because of the
conduct of the students at the Royal University Con-
ferring Day. Our Belfast contemporary commences its
first leading article in Saturday’s issue in this way:—
“We hope the Senate of the Royal University will take
serious notice of the disgraceful conduct of a number of
Nationalist undergraduates at the annual conferring of
degrees yesterday. It is usual for the students of most
universities on such occasions to allow themselves con-
siderable latitude, but as a rule their conduct is marked
by good humour, and the university. authorities take
little notice of it. But an organised display of ruf-
fianism and disloyalty such as that which is described in
another column is not to be tolerated, and it is sur-
prising that persons who have commenced a university
career could have been guilty of it.”” 3

Here are a few more amusing extracts from the irate
organ of the lamb-like Orangemen : —* The musical pro-
gramme which precedes the ceremony was interrupted,
and some of the ring’leaders tried to raise ‘ The Boys of
Wexford.” This was an indication of the object for
B which the disturbance had been organised. They came

; to prove that they were animated by no mild disaffec-
tion, but by disloyalty of the true Fenian type.”” And
again:— About thirty of the rowdies violently took
possession of the organ gallery. The object, as they
afterwards avowed, was to prevent the playing of the

National Anthem at the close of the proceedings, and in

this they were successful.”” The poor old horror-stricken

and tear bedewed leading article writer in the News

Letter records painfully that ¢ The retreat of the organitd

after closing the keyboard, delighted them, and they
. proceeded to rejoice over their victory.”

The mild-eyed Orange leading article writer in his
inquiries as to who these ‘“rebels’’ were, remarks:—
We have no doubt that the influence of the Gaelic
League also contributed to the disgraceful scene. That
‘organisation is notoriously disloyal. Its aim is to sub-
Irish for English as the language of the people,
Itimately to destroy every trace of the connection

public, or an independent Ireland with a descendant of

some of the old tribal chiefs as King.” ‘We had in-

We have received a copy of the catalogue of the Aonac
organised by the London Gaelic League, which will
be held on next Saturday and next Monday at the
Bishopsgate Institute, Bishopsgate Street Without. We .
hope that our large circle of London readers will, }f
possible, and whether they are members of thg (_}aehc

third Annual Aonach. Last year there wero 22 stalls; i
this year there are 32. London, to say nothing of Britain B

generally, offers a wide market for many Irish products.
The Irish populations in the various centres where any
Irish Industrial Organisation exists, present a favour-
able field for certain Irish goods and once they get a
grip they can be pushed practically indefinitely on their
merits. Our friends in the United States who desire
to support Irish goods, are confronted with heavy tariffs
over which Irish goods have to climb before they are
allowed to compete in the American market, whilst if
perfidious Albion has opened our ports to the world
without consulting us, at least her ports are open to us:
and we should make the most of the situation.

Up to this we have refrained from making any re-
ference to the unfortunate scandal at Portarlington in
connection with the local branch of the Gaelic League.
Now that the formal enquiry has been held and the
Bishop has given his decision, it remains to be seen
what action, if any, the Executive will take with the
local branch.

The Parish Priest of Portarlington objected to mixed
night classes in the town, a town which we gather is not
publicly lighted. An afternoon Irish class for girls
was formed, under a competent teacher, in the Convent
School. The Parish Priest advised - parents to send
their daughters to the afternoon classes at the Convent.
In the course of his remarks he said that there was no i
need for girls to be out at night if it were nothing but
Gaelic they wanted.

e

Now the country is strongly against the mixed classes
scheme of the ‘“ National >’ Education Board, so that it
is not surprising to find a Parish Priest advising the
young girls of Portarlington to go to the afternoon
Irish class provided by the nuns rather than a mixed
night class. All these things have to be taken on their
merits and in connection with attending circumstances. 5
But anyway a Parish Priest is on his own ground if he 4=
decides to take up a position in connection with a
matter involving mixed night classes for young people.

It appears that one at least of the Gaelic Leaguers
got very excited and read volumes into the remark that
there was no need for girls to be out at night if it was
nothing but Gaelic they wanted. Some people will get:
excited over anything; we can see nothing in the re-
mark to boil up about. Anyhow the local Gaelic
League promptly expelled the Parish Priest from the
branch of which, it appears, he was not a member. On
the following Sunday the Curate referred to the matter,
a prominent man in the local League got up in his
place in church, and talked back to the priest. A
pretty picture in an Irish church! Policemen were -
called in and then things quieted down.

Needless to say tactics like these do not help the
‘Gaelic League in an Irish town, or anywhere else, and
the enemies the League has to face are many :

powerful enough without creating unnecessary diffi -

like hot cakes, being out of print within two days after

No wonder the Sourfaces are frightened of the in-
tellect of Ireland; no wonder they put out all their
strength on the side of obscurantism. The Sourface
policy of attempting by political dishonesty to obscure,
as far as such methods can do so, the intellect of “ Ido-
latrous ** Treland is a petty policy, but it is in keeping
with the littleness of that exotic breed who live on
this country as England’s Faithful Garrison.

And with all their bigotry and trickery and dis-
honesty the Royal University Results, like the Inter-
mediate results, are sufficient to terrify them, and
ought to be more than sufficient to make them ashamed.
University College, where Jesuits, quite undisguised,
prowl around, gained 76 Distinctions as against 9 by
the highly endowed Queen’s College, Cork; Loretto
College, Dublin, gained 41 Distinctions. If this
-““Idolatrous *’ College had gained one more distinction
it would have gained exactly double the combined
totals of those two houses of enlightenment with the

= royal names, Alexandra College and Victoria College.
Alexandra gained only eleven distinctions and Victoria
College only ten. St. Mary’s College, Dublin, gained
twenty-six, which is five more than the combined totals
of the Alexandra and Victoria.

Corrigan and French, will include, besides their

Humphrey's Dump, according to its organ, the Inde-
pendent, has received the “ warm approval *’ of the
Trades Hall, Capel Street, where the bar is. The
Trades Council expressed the hope that Humphrey’s
Dump would commence being dumped in Dublin with-
out delay. It would appear that the International
Committee played their cards cleverly, if unscrupulously,
with the Trades Council deputation. One of the latter,
Mr. Lumsden, told Capel Street that the deputation
“were treated very kindly and courteously.”” The In-
ternationalists told the deputation from Capel Street
that the object in carrying out the exhibition was to
give employment to the workers of Dublin. That auda-
cious misrepresentation may have  deceived the Trades
Hall people, but it could not deceive any person of
ordinary shrewdness unless they weakly wished to be
deceived. In the end, the following preposterous reso-
lution concerning this obnoxious and anti-Irish scheme
was unanimously adopted at the meeting of the Capel
Street Trades Council :—* That this Council, in thank-
ing the deputation for their services, approves of the
satisfactory assurances given by the Exhibition Com-
mittee regarding the employment of Irish material and
local labour under trade union conditions in the erec-
tion of the Exhibition Buildings; and expresses the
carnest hope that, in view of the abnormal dearth of
employment at present prevailing in the city, the Ex-
hibition Committee may be enabled to place the con-
tract in the hands of the contractors without delay.’”’
And thus has the Trades Council been purchased by the
crumbs that are anticipated to fall from the table of
Humphrey’s Dump that is calculated to do incalculable
injury to Irish industry. Perhaps some of the Trades
Council will be knighted in due course! Let us
once again quote from a recent circular issued by
the promoters of this selfish and anti-Irish scheme:—
“Such an exhibition will most certainly receive the
support of the merchants, producers, manufacturers,
and_others from all parts of the world who are
anxious to obtain a portion of the trade of-Ireland.’
There is a great deal of Sinn Féin about that! We
suppose dealers in imported prayer books, bottles, join-
ery work, shop fittings, and the rest will be busy during
the holding of Humphrey’s Dump—if it ever comes off
—attempting to further “ obtain a portion of the trade
of Ireland’’ on these lines. And when the Trades
Council people’ have eaten Humphrey’s crumbs they
will then resume their occupation of resoluting agaiust
those traders who have given orders to the “ merchants,
producers, manufacturers and others from all parts of
the world,” who anxious “to obtain portion of the
trade of Ireland,”’ took full advantage of the cheap op-
portunity afforded them by Humphrey’s Dump.

The Convent of Mercy, Tuam, is to be congratulated
on the result of the recent inter-school competition under
the auspices of the Tuam Gaelic League which has just
been announced. The Convent has succeeded in winning
the coveted “M‘Hale Silver Cup’’ awarded to the schools
securing the highest aggregate marks in these examina-
tions.  In addition to this recognition the school will
also receive an illuminated certificate in Irish, a £1 prize
for being first in third Division, and each of its pupils
presented will be awarded a money prize.

. We are glad to hear that this Convent has been very
successful with its ﬂ:ecial class for candidates preparing
for the Teachers’ Examination. ~Within the last few
years many candidates have passed with a high per-
centage of marks at the Teachers’ Easter Examinations.

By permission of his Grace Most Rev. Dr. Walsh,
Axchbishop of Dublin, the annual charity sermon in aid
of the funds of the Sick and Indigent Roomkeepers’
Society, will be preached in the Pro-Cathedral, Marl-
borough Street, on next Sunday at last Mass. Rev.
Thomas J. Fogarty, C.S. Sp., will preach on the occa-
sion. Special collections will be made that day in all
the Catholic churches of the city for the same purpose.

N Saturday,” October 21st, the centenary of the

destruction of the French fleet in Trafalgar Bay,

the Unionists of Dublin celebrated the Entente Cor-

diale by decorating Nelson’s Pillar with cheap imported

shoddy in the shape of Union Jacks, so that the monu-

ment looked like a gigantic scare crow put up to frighten
away the mere Irish from such consecrated ground.

. NoveMBER 4, 1905.

On Nelson when the sun was low
No shoddy rags waved to and fro,
No Union Jacks or calico .
Of England’s Faithful Garrison.

But Nelson was another sight

Next morning in the broad day light,

Decked out with ha’penny streamers bright
By England’s Faithful Garrison.

In dumpers shoddy bright arrayed ¥
Great Nelson standing with his blade
With stony stare in front surveyed

Old England’s Faithful Garrison.

Before his vision loomed a few
Tough sea dogs of the briny blue,
Jack tars belonging to the crew ’
Of England’s Faithful Garrison.

There stood Alf Foz and also Buff,
Right hearty lads both hale and bluff;
Old salts who saw some service rough

In England’s Faithful Garrison.

And Brother Goulding, good old Bill,

A jolly tar sea-worthy still,

‘Who holds the Kingsbridge with good will
For England’s Faithful Garrison.

To them spoke Nelson loud and full,
Heave ho, my lads, a long, long pull,
And altogether for John Bull

And England’s Faithful Garrison.

Well done, my hearties, hoist away,
Brace up your dumper’s shoddy gay.
Your duty you have done to-day
For England and the Garrison.
AM.W.

FAITH AND FAMINE.

IT was remarked to us on Saturday night, “ When
you don’t like the play and the audience, at
least you might say a good word for the Youghal Mir-
rors.”” The. Youghal Mirrors are certainly an abiding
beauty at the Abbey Theatre, and -we admired them
on Saturday night; we also, and that was a rare ex-
perience, liked the audience and the play. There was
a fairly good crowd present, but as the theatre is small,
it should on this special occasion have been full ; indeed,
it would not be too much to have expected that a’few
hundreds had perforce to be turned from the doors.
The prices, 3s. 2s. and 1s. are unworkable, particularly
for Irish plays that have yet to compete against so
much -apathy and ignorance; 2s. 1s. and 6d. would be
what we might call the natural prices under the cir-
cumstances. - Of course the Keating Branch of the
Gaelic League were powerless in this matter, for those
who control this “ uncommercial ’ theatre founded os-
tensibly for the purpose of developing dramatic art in
Dublin, have ordained that no one is “class’’ enough
for Art below the shilling line.  As for the spooky and
Syngy Plays we fear indeed that the authorities, if they
wish for full houses, will eventually be driven to gwe
3s., 2s., and 1s. to those members of the public who
may be induced to contract to sit them out. But there
were no spooks ‘or Synginess at the Abbey Theatre on
Saturday night, and it was a pity that the prices could
not have been rationalised from the “classy’’ heights
to 2s., 1s., and 6d. ?
It was an Irish night, and it was an Irish audience.
The stalls looked kindly Irish of the Irish, neither sour
nor Syngy. One felt that he was not intruding into
a Sunday school where he probably would have to listen
to-an_allegory. for the good of his unoffending soul,
One had a comfortable feeling that he was of the
audience and-not a- sort: of outsider-taking mental and
unsympathetic notes: . - Where wer the: coterie of the
Anglo-Irish theatre® <= . it
- There are some few gooq -peopl
in this country though their

being Irish; they play seriously—they are nathing if
not serious—indeed they are as serious as children ower
the game, and often about as sensible. They were not
present, on Saturday night, for the play was about
Soupers. When those of the Garrison breed who would
be Irish can enter with sympathy into the feelings of
the country with regard to the Soupers, there may be
some hope for them throwing in their lot with Ireland.
Father Dinneen’s play, Créroeam asup Sopcy,
turned out to be a better acting play than we had anti-
cipated ; indeed it is a very good acting play, and the
actors did their parts well. “Oonncao Ua he€almgte
as Smclea»rv the Souper Enlightener-in-chief to starving
obscurantists,” did very well, although one could pic-
ture a more forcible rendering of the part; the other
two principal parts were acted excellently. The acting
of Seagdn Sonm was quite a first-class piece of work,
and the same may be said of Miss Sighle Hallisey’s
Caic ni Suilteabdin. The Abbey Street Theatre—we
mean the Anglo-Irish one—has trained up an efficient
company of actors, but the Keating Branch of the
Gaelic League in the acting ability displayed in Cpéro-
eam agup Sopca could hold their own with them and
that is saying something. Those whom we have wit-
nessed interpreting Irish peasant women at the Abbey
Theatre leave more to be desired than did Miss Hal-
lisey’s excellent reading of the part of Cdic m Suil-
Lteabdin. Certainly the Keating Branch ought to be
able to train up a Company that could do admirable
work all over the country for Irish (without the Anglo)
drama. We have not seen all the Irish plays that have
been produced in recent years, but Cpéioeam agup
Sopcais decidedly the best we have seen. It touched
the audience, for it moved many to tears as well as to
emotions that do not express themselves in tears. The
scene is laid in the time of the Famine when Protestant
ghouls were prowling about the stricken land looking
for souls in exchange for soup. It is a fit subject for
drama, and the author rose to the subject. We have no
sympathy with those tame people who object to treating
this subject for fear of hurting the susceptibilities of
those, some of whom are at the present moment fish-
ing amongst the Papists of the Intermediate for black-
legs for Sinclear’s University. Why didn’t the Protes-
tants cry out against the ghoulish trafic? ~Why don’t
they rise up against the milder manifestations of it to-
day in the Belfast Corporation, the ‘Great Northern
Railway, the Provincial Bank, in the Robber Univer-
sity? ~ There is still plenty of soup—soup for the
“ Saved ’—being ladled out. The “Saved ’’ make be-
lieve to seek Truth; well then give it to them by all
*  means even if their sanctimonious eyes must look—that
is if they would go to see it—at little children dying
of starvation because they refused to barter their Faith
for the Souper’s pay. If we are to have sincerity, then
in the name of truth let us have sincerity all round.
Cpéroeath agup Jorca is.a powerful play and faces
the facts of Irish life with which it deals with that
sincerity without which plays are only charades for chil-
dren. It is sad and it awakens emotions that some
weak people may think had better be left lie. Irish
history, however it might please our enemies, is not for
us to forget. Has the Souper of to-day cchanged his
spots as well as his methods? Sinclear may not be
doling soup so plentifully to-day; but is he not doling
“education,’” is he not making Freemason signs? Is
he not proposing to rob teachers of Irish of some
£12,000 annually in fees. Sinclear’s'soup:cools, but his
spirit keeps simmering on.

The scene in which ‘Caic n1 ‘Suilleabéin watches
her three children dying ‘because ‘they would mot sell
their Faith for soup belongs to all ‘time and to all
countries ; it is a scene from the great eternal drama.
It was admirably staged -and acted. Those whose centre
of gravity may be in this‘world might consider the an-
gel’s voices as fraudulent as'“faked * butter and there-
fore false art. We do not think so. That scene is
enacted every day in the year in Ireland. It is not
three children \who ‘are dying amid the harmony. of

IRISH IRELAND, LITERATURE, AND SCORN.

AM glad that Mr. Victory raised that question

about our scorn of English literature, for I en-
joyed both his article and the replies it drew forth.
Whether or not controversy be the best method of
getting at that shy bird, Truth, I do not care to dis-
cuss, for I am satisfied, at all events, that controversy
is better than apathy. But I don’t think Mr. Victory
should have used the word “scorn.”  Such of us as
read English literature do not scorn it, and such as
scorn it without reading it don’t count in the world of
mind. Scorn of anything usually implies love of its
contrary, and if any of our people scorn English litera-
ture it is hardly because of their love for Irish litera-
ture. Besides, scorn is something much too active for
many of our people; they are, for a large part, too
apathetic to be scornful. Indeed I should wager that,
if you pressed the average Irish Philistine to read Eng-
lish literature, he would reduce you to the same silence
and despair as he would a Gaelic Leaguer who asked
him to study Irish, and with the same old question:
‘“ What good would it do me?’” For an unpractical
people we have a wonderful turn {or utilitarianism!

What I think Mr. Victory should have done was to
raise the wider issue of our distaste for culture. As a
people we do not scorn English literature, nor any other
literature ; we simply do not read literature at all. I
think there must not be many countries where so few
books are bought, and so few read, as in Ireland; the
newspaper dominates all, and the brain-rotting process
goes on under its unceasing influence. ~ Through the
medium of the newspaper there is a constant infiltration
of whatever is cheapest and commonest in English life
and thought, and our people’s minds are vitiated, not
through scorn of English literature, but through love
of English garbage, and, perhaps, Irish garbage too.
In the conclusion of his article, ““ C.”” hits upon the
great cause of our backwardness in culture: the want
of a centre of higher learning for Catholic Ire-
land; in other words, the want of a University of
which Irish Ireland can avail itself with confidence.
ITowever, the culture question and the University ques-
tion are rather outside the issue raised by Mr. Victory; -
hence T must leave them alone.

Upon one point I think Mr. Victory is not quite just
to the Irish Trelanders, that is, in attributing to them
an indifference to literature. Had the original Gaelic
Leaguers been wunliterate, or anti-literary men, they
would mever have stanted the Gaelic League. Indeed,
I fancy that all the living culture, or aspiration to-
wards culture, existing in Catholic Ireland to-day be-
longs to the Gaelic Leaguers; outside these ranks, at
all events outside the Irish Treland ranks, any culture
that exists .at all is not a living culture, but a deaa!
growthless, academic affair, which will never do any-'
thing for Ireland. 'There, for instance, is Mr. Dowdenj
he does not scorn English literature. Yet I maintain
that Mr. Dowden, writing a book on ‘Browning,'-h'aidl
presents himself to the mind’s eye ‘as -an Irishman
culture.  Douglas Hyde, and Father Dinneen,
Father ‘O'Leary are Irishmen of culture, ‘whereas,
should say that the great Atkinson is noti-one,-since &
‘man -can ‘hardly ‘be anti-Irish, and yet claim fo-

Irishman of culture. I think the real scorners of lite-
rature, English and other, will be found outside the
ranks of Irish Ireland.

One w?iter raises the question of Catholicity with re-

gard to literature, apparently requiring that our people
should read no literature save what is distinctly Catho-
lic.. This I shouldp@ll & curious restriction, and one to
which, uncompromising Catholic though I am, I should
never submit. May we not read Plato then, or Homer?
Surely these were not Catholic, and yet no one dare
{ < suggest that we should not read them, unless he wanted
5 to get laughed at. The late Lord Acton was a Catholic
(I understand), and yet I should not give his ‘‘Letters”
to any young Catholic to read, nor, I think, to any
Catholic. The only thing we have the right to require
5 of literature is that it shall be literature ; that it shall
be a true and beautiful account of man’s life and as-
pirations. Hardly any large book, or work, is litera-
ture throughout; Wordsworth’s ‘“ Excursion ’’ is com-
posed of much tedious, prosy stuff—even of downright
rubbish, occasionally—relieved at intervals with good
bits and divine gleams. These latter make the litera-
ture.  Just as Wordsworth’s poorer work is spotted
with rubbish, so are Scott’s novels spotted with bigotry ;
but_the bigotry is not literature. The ‘‘ danger ” of
reading these things is greatly exaggerated; it is sup-
posed that the author’s style will make the poison tell,
whereas the fact is that bigotry is never fascinating,
and cannot be made so. However, if Catholic litera-
ture is essential, read it by all means. The late Denis
Florence McCarthy executed some fine translations
from Calderon, but his countrymen don’t care for that
kind of thing. ‘“ What good would it do them?’’
What do they want with Calderon while they have the
Daily Brainrotter and the Sporting Pimp? Alas, cen-
turies of persecution and confiscation prevent us from
reading the cheap editions of the classics! How can
we buy books while an alien government forces us to
spend £14,000,000 a year on spirituous refreshments ?
It can’t be done!

The question of the extent to which Irish Ireland
should study foreign literatures deserves thought. - We
cannot be cultured if we ignore these literatures; if we
give ourselves too much to them, we may lose our own
distinctiveness and individuality. These latter attri-
‘butes gone, or impoverished, how shall we create a great
literature of our own? Yet, without some culture,
what standard of excellence can we have? Iow can
we judge what advance we are making? Then, too, the
Gaelic League propagandists insist on the value of the
mental quickening caused by being bi-lingual. Well,
if it is an advantage to be bi-lingual, is it also an ad-
4 -vantage to remain uncultured in the literature of either
2 of the two languages? Surely not. Culture in both
languages and their literatures is the ideal to aim at.
Literary culture is now an admitted essential of higher
.education, and its need requires no demonstrating. Re-
membering, however, how well Homer and Shakespeare
got along with one language, let us not fall into the
lerror of thinking that literary culbure is everything.
Never did the world so want an absolutely fresh litera-
ture, and the more we are thrown on our own resources,
the more chance, perhaps, we may .huve of producing
it. - Meanwhile, however, reading literature would be

“better than bar-loafing, betting, or spouting raimeis.

spirit of Gladstone. The Liberal party will to-day as
in the days of the past, make as few concessions to the
Irish people as self-interest will permit them to do.
They are probably as little moved by consideration of
abstract justice as are the present Russian Government
in granting administrative reforms. No doubt English
Liberals have, like most other people, and will always
continue to have a body of abstract moral principles to
which they swear allegiance; but it is practical expe-
diency that determines which particular principles they
shall at any given moment apply on any given occasion,
whether for instance they shall, at any given
moment, devote a session of Parliament to tem-
perance or to justice. If, therefore, we find
the Liberals at present tending to specialise on qualities
in which Irishmen are but slightly interested, we must
not blame them or accuse them of moral turpitude; we
should rather recognise that their moral nature is still
unchanged, and devote ourselves to creating through
parliamentary or other means such an atmosphere of ex-
pediency as may be suitable for the development of their
latent virtues.

In this matter of manceuvring with the Liberals, a
rather heavy responsibility lies on the leaders of the
Irish party. I do not intend to enter into the tiresome
details of the O’Brien affair; but this much stands out
clear. O’Brien staked his position on an alliance with
the Conservatives, out of which he probably would have
got something, though very possibly much less than he
expected. The present leaders of the party opposed
him in that policy and O’Brien was ruined in the result,
and, of course, whatever he might have obtained from
such line of action, lost. Very probably they were
right in taking this course and making whatever tem-
porary sacrifice was involved in waiting for redress from
a Liberal government. But having made the sacrifice,
whatever it amounted to, a strong duty lies on the Irish
leaders to get at least as much, if not a great deal more,
out of the Liberals than O’Brien could have got out of
the Tories. The campaign of Mr. O’Brien at the pre-
sent moment is singularly unfortunate, though emi-
nently natural. Mr. O’Brien feels that he was unfairly
jockeyed out of the Irish leadership, without his policy
having been fairly submitted to the county. For this
his own retirement was of course largely responsible,
but like King James in a similar situation, he naturally
comes back to try the issue in open war. Never, how-
ever, was an occasion more unhappily selected. What
Mr. O’Brien does not see is that whilst his policy was
essentially that of a Tory alliance, there are, for the
moment, no Tories to ally with, and, therefore, even
if the whole country came round to his side his policy
could not just at present advance one foot. The exi-
gencies of English politics make it necessary that our
dealings should be with the Liberals for some time to
come ; then, if the Irish party should fail, Mr. O’Brien
would have an excellent opportunity for fighting his
battle upon a policy of “I told you so.”’

The great difficulties of the Irish members in treat-
ing with the Liberals are, on the one side, the expected
great strength of the Liberal party, and on the other the
terrible difficulty of the English Education Act. The
latter is far the worse of the two. In this matter Mr.
Balfour has with his characteristic cleverness manceuvred
the Irish party into a very nasty corner and put them
in an essentially false position. They are committed to
oppose the one measure on which their prospective
Liberal allies have their hearts set, and this a measure
which in no way affects the interests of their Irish con-
stituents. It is very difficult to see how unless they can
arrive at some compromise with the Liberals, they are
to surmount the obstacle. The English Catholic Tory
must in truth contemplate the situation with sarcastic
satisfaction. S s g

(2). And now consider the effect on the music. Only

loveliest melodies might as well not exist, while many

all, are heard ad nauseam because they happen to be
fitted with Irish words. And here appears another in-
«consistency—the words must be Irish, but apparently

land,” a second-rate American tune;  The Dear Little
‘Shamrock,”” a modern music-hall ditty; and several of
Lover’s airs, which are Irish and water?

Another limitation arises from the fact that not every
good Irish speaker is a good singer. Hence Irish
speakers who are but indifferent singers are put forward,
to the great injury both of the music and of the Gaelic
League concerts. I learn that these concerts are losing
ground in many quarters. I'm not surprised. People
will not continue to go again and again to hear the same
songs and the same singers however good—still less if, as
often happens, neither song nor singer is worth hearing.

(3). Finally, I assert that the present practice of the
Gaelic League is likely to have a disastrous effect on the
whole future of Irish song. For what is our greatest
want in this department of the Irish Revival? Is it not
an adequate supply of racy Gaelic songs for our old airs
—songs full of native thought and feeling, lit up with
native imagery, such as will appeal strongly and directly
to the native heart and mind? And how are such songs
to be produced except by filling the air of Ireland with
the music that is waiting for them? A poet,”” says
the old adage, ““is born, not made.””  Like many an-
other old saw, this is but a half a truth. The whole
truth is that a poet is born and made—born with the
poetic gift within, but shaped and determined by his
environment. Take the case of Burns, a typical
people’s poet. It was undoubtedly his extensive know-
ledge of the old Scotch airs that made him so. He
learned them to any words; and when the fime came for
his own genius to speak, they were there to invite and
guide its outpourings. If he had not known the old airs
the Scots might be still waiting for their favourite songs.
So will it be with the ballad poet of Irish Ireland. If
his heart is full of the old melodies, he will sing his
songs to them—not otherwise. Therefore, I repeat, fill
the air of Ireland with the old music to amy words.
There is no time to lose. Our ballad poet may be even
now in the cradle or in a Gaelic League class. It is for
us to provide the musical environment that will make
him what we so urgently want—the interpreter in song

of the mind and heart of Irish Ireland.

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT.—XI.

HAVE shown in the last article, that, notwith-
‘standing Cromwell’s death, and the change of
government therefrom ensuing, the Trustees appointed

by the Deed were in legal possession of the lands given
by the Deed, under the trusts therein mentioned, and
set forth. I have also shown, that it was altogether
beyond the power of Erasmus Smith to change any of
those trusts set forth in, and by, the Deed. Yet, after
the Act of Settlement passed in 1662, Erasmus Smith
sold the trust lands, in the Isles of Arran, to the Earl
of Arran. This sale is mentioned and legally sanctioned
by the 135th section of the Act of Explanation, passed
in 1665. And in the Bill, framed by Erasmus Smith,
dated the 13th day of May, 1664, and sent by him
into England, he changed some of the trusts, and added
others. He appointed £100 per annum to be paid to
Christ’s Hospital, London, and part of the endowment
to be expended on clothing and apprenticing the poor
scholars in the schools; and he reduced the number of
Free Schools from five to three, knocked out the Free
Schools to be built at Sligo and Tipperary—the School
to be built at Dunluce, county Antrim, was probably
knocked out by the land being restored to the former
owner—left fixed only the Galway Free School, and
reserved to himself the determination of the sites of the
remaining two Schools. He limited the tenants’ child-
ren entitled to free education to those living within
two English miles of the three Free Schools. During
the time of his lease, he appointed £50 per annum to
he devoted to supporting five English schools, or to
some other charitable purpose to be fixed by himself.
He increased the salaries of the schoolmasters of the

three Grammar Schools from £40 to £66 13s. 4d. a _

year. . He changed the exhibitions in Trinity College,
to be given to the scholars in the three Free Schools,
from £10 a year for four years to £8 a year for seven
years. He, moreover, reserved to himself power to
‘devote an - undefined moiety of surplus to other
charitable uses to be appointed by himself. As
I have said, Erasmus Smith had no
whatever to make these or any other changes in the
Trusts. But did the 85th section of the Act of Ex-
_planation make those changes in the trusts? It would
‘appear that it did. For it says:—'‘ Whereas . . .

power

NovEMBER 4.° 190'5"._

a bill is transmitted ”’ (into England) ‘ intituled An
Act For Settling Certain Lands of Erasmus Smith,
Esq. For Charitable Uses; beit further enacted. . .
That all the lands, &c., in said bill mentioned, and
thereby intended to be disposed for charitable uses, and
not already decreed away. . shall be continued
and applied unto, and preserved intirely for such pious
and charitable uses.”” That is, the lands not decreed
away are to be continued for suck pious uses as are
mentioned in the bill. Then after commanding the
Commissioners not to give away to any soldier, or ad-
venturer any part of those lands now in the possession
of the Trustees, the section adds:—‘‘ And where any
of the lands set out by the said Erasmus Smith to pious
or charitable uses, have been evicted, recovered, or
charged by any decrees which are confirmed by this
Act, the Commissioners . . of this Act shall cause a
like quantity of forfeited and profitable acres within
the county of Lowth, if so much can there be found, or
otherwise elsewhere, to be set and allotted to the same
pious uses, as may be sufficient to recompense the loss
which hath happened by such decrees aforesaid.”” Here
again, the land that is to be given in recompense for the
land taken away is to be allotted to the same pious uses
as those mentioned in the bill.

The three Certificates of the Court of Claims, and the
Letters Patent of 1667 tell us that the pious uses men-
tioned in the Bill sent into England are the same as
those given and set forth in those Certificates and in
the Letters Patent of 1667. The Commissioners of the
Court of Claims have decreed in each of the Cerfifi-
cates that the Trustees are entitled to the lands for the
pious uses mentioned in the Bill sent into England,
and that these pious uses are the same as those which
they enumerate in the Certificates. /

As the 85th section of the Act of Explanation had
reduced the number of Free Schools to three, and had

I\It}KE it a practice never to reply to anonymous
Y disputants. I signed my article on the above sub-
ject, and I do not see why “‘ Fionnbarr”’ and C.’”
shonl_d not do likewise. If a man has ideas to express,
and is not afraid to express them, why hide behind a
pseudonym ?
. However, the points raised are so interesting and so
important that it would be a pity to let the discussion
end now.

‘‘ Fionnbarr ’ thinks he scores a point against me
when he states that the ‘“ National *’ school-books are
crammed with extracts from the beauties of English
literature to the exclusion of all things Irish, and that
the Intermediate Board and the R.U.I. compel the
study of English literature. But I would ask, how
many of the National School pupils and how many of
the Intermediate and R.U.I. pupils carry to the period
of adolescence or of manhood any recollection of the
English classics which they have studied? My experi-
ence teaches me that the said classics are utterly for-
gotten as soon as the examination-room is out of sight.

In any event, the National Schools’, the Inter-
mediate and the R.U.I. systems of education cannot be
taken as indicative in any way of the educational aspira-
tions of any considerable section of Irishmen; so that
they scarcely come properly under the present dis-
cussion.

The vast majority of Irish adults know nothing what-
ever about English literature, in spite of the alleged
Anglicising efforts of the educational institutions men-
tioned by your correspondent; and that regrettable fact
cannot be gainsaid.

“ Fionnbarr ’’ does not think that my strictures
refer to the ‘ Yeats group.’”” In this he is right. T do
not find anything Irish in the work of this group: I
find in it strong influences of ‘‘ The Secret Doctrine,’’
the ‘‘ Upanishads,’’ the French symbolists, and Ibsen.

The objections of ‘‘ Fionnbarr * to the study of Eng-
lish literature by Irishmen are, to my mind, paltry and
futile. He raises the issue (which should not be raised
in connection with literature, because it is a non-
literary issue) which has been the curse of this country
—the differences between Catholic and Protestant.
This religious issue has nothing whatever to do with
literature: it is a question for those who have an axe
to grind and whose desire it is to keep alive the fires of
dissension in Ireland.

By the way, why does not your correspondent, com-
plain of the Pagan influences in the Irish MSS.?

““ Tongue ”’ and ‘‘ atmosphere ’’ are side issues of the

literary question: if a work be literature (that is, if it.

have the eternal elements which appeal to humanity
wherever humanity may be found) it matters not in
what ‘ tongue’’ or in what ‘‘ atmosphere ’’ it had its
origin. ‘¢ What is to be thought of the prescription of
master writers that Mr. Louis H. Victory prepares for
us?’’ queries ‘‘ Fionnbarr *’; ‘and he proceeds to sneer
at some of the master-intellects of the world, men
whose writings have the universal human appeal, and
the fine intellectual presentment, which are the posses-
sion of all lasting literature, men whose work shall be
an influence among peoples yet unborn.
would he substitute for their masterpieces? Newman,
Montalembert, Bossuet, Fenelon! There is no univer-
sal human appeal in the work of these writers. Con-
sequently, their writings are not literature of a high

Irish.

““ Fionnbarr ”’ sneers at North’s translation of Plu-

incorporating long passages therefrom in “ Antony and
Cleopatra,”” ““ Julius Casar,’”” and other plays, making
such slight changes in the wording as the exigencies of
his metre demanded.

Then again, Hollinshed is not so formless as any-
